chunk_summary: Feeding strategies for infants with cleft lip or palate focus on ensuring adequate nutrition, supporting breastfeeding when possible, using specialized feeding devices, and monitoring growth until surgical repair.
learning_objectives: Understand feeding challenges faced by infants with cleft lip and/or palate.
Learn techniques to optimize feeding, including use of cleft-adapted devices and positioning.
Recognize signs of feeding distress and when to seek specialized care.
Explore complementary feeding strategies for infants post-cleft repair.
key_facts: Infants with cleft palate often struggle with suction, making direct breastfeeding ineffective.
Specialty cleft bottles with one-way valves or spouted cups (e.g., paladai) can aid feeding in low-resource settings.
Feeds should generally be completed within 30 minutes; prolonged feeds may indicate the need for intervention.
Thicker complementary foods (e.g., thick purees) are recommended to prevent nasal regurgitation.
Frequent burping and upright feeding (45-degree angle) reduce air swallowing and distress.
reference_quote: Specialty cleft bottles with one-way valves or caregiver-assisted flow reduce the need for suction; in low-resource Indian settings, a paladai (spouted cup) with careful pacing can be an alternative. — Seattle Children’s
document_key_topics: Feeding challenges in cleft lip and palate
